title: Geometry of the Complex Plane and Polar Form

epigraph.text: He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set eternity in the human heart.
epigraph.quote: God made the integers; all else is the work of man.

content.text:
The complex numbers form a two-dimensional space, and this geometric 
viewpoint is essential to understanding their behavior. In this section 
we explore the **complex plane** (also called the **Argand 
plane** or **Gaussian plane**) and develop the 
polar representation of complex numbers.

We represent the complex number $z = x + iy$ as the point $(x, y)$ in 
$\R^2$. The horizontal axis is called the **real axis** 
(corresponding to real numbers), and the vertical axis is called the 
**imaginary axis** (corresponding to purely 
imaginary numbers of the form $iy$).

items: Addition of complex numbers corresponds to vector addition.
The modulus $|z|$ is the distance from the origin to $z$.
The conjugate $\conj{z}$ is the reflection of $z$ across the real axis.
The quantity $|z - w|$ is the distance between points $z$ and $w$.

content.title: Circles and lines in $\C$
content.problem:
The equation $|z - z_0| = r$ describes a circle of radius $r$ centered at 
$z_0$. The equation $|z - a| = |z - b|$ describes the perpendicular bisector 
of the segment from $a$ to $b$.
content.solution:
For the circle: $|z - z_0| = r$ means the distance from $z$ to $z_0$ is $r$, 
which is the definition of a circle.

For the line: $|z - a| = |z - b|$ means $z$ is equidistant from $a$ and $b$, 
which defines the perpendicular bisector.

content.text:
Just as points in $\R^2$ can be described by Cartesian coordinates $(x, y)$ 
or polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$, complex numbers have a polar 
representation.

content.title: Polar Form of a Complex Number
content.content:
A nonzero complex number $z$ can be written as
\[
 z = r(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta),
\]
where $r = |z| > 0$ and $\theta$ is an **argument** of 
$z$—an angle from the positive real axis to the line segment from $0$ to $z$.

content.text:
The argument is not unique: if $\theta$ is an argument, so is $\theta + 2\pi k$ 
for any integer $k$. We define the **principal argument** 
$\Arg(z)$ to be the unique argument in the interval $(-\pi, \pi]$.

The relationship between Cartesian and polar forms is:
\[
 x = r\cos\theta, \quad y = r\sin\theta, \quad 
 r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \arctan(y/x) \text{ (with care for quadrant)}.
\]

One of the most beautiful results in all of mathematics connects the 
exponential function to trigonometry.

content.title: Euler's Formula
content.content:
For any real number $\theta$,
\[
 e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
We will prove this rigorously when we study the complex exponential in 
Chapter 3. For now, we take it as a definition of what $e^{i\theta}$ means.

Using Euler's formula, the polar form becomes:
\[
 z = re^{i\theta}.
\]
This is called the **exponential form** of a 
complex number.

content.title: Converting between forms
content.solution:
We have $r = |z| = \sqrt{1 + 3} = 2$. For the argument:
\[
 \cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \sin\theta = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2},
\]
so $\theta = \pi/3$. Thus $z = 2e^{i\pi/3}$.

content.text: Multiplication and Division in Polar Form
The polar form reveals the geometric meaning of complex multiplication.

content.title: Multiplication in Polar Form
content.content:
If $z_1 = r_1 e^{i\theta_1}$ and $z_2 = r_2 e^{i\theta_2}$, then
\[
 z_1 z_2 = r_1 r_2 e^{i(\theta_1 + \theta_2)}.
\]
That is, we **multiply the moduli** and **add the arguments**.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$$
 z_1 z_2 &= r_1(\cos\theta_1 + i\sin\theta_1) \cdot r_2(\cos\theta_2 + i\sin\theta_2) \\
 &= r_1 r_2 [(\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2) 
 + i(\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 + \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2)] \\
 &= r_1 r_2 [\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + i\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)]
$$
by the angle addition formulas.

content.text:
This is remarkable: multiplication by a complex number $w = se^{i\phi}$ 
*rotates* by angle $\phi$ and *scales* by factor $s$. Complex 
multiplication is rotation-dilation!

marginNotes.text:
It is he who sits above the circle of the earth, 
and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers.

marginNotes.author: Jean-Robert Argand (1806)
marginNotes.text:
Every quantity, positive or negative, 
can be represented geometrically.
